Selected Work

Case Studies

Case Study / 01

Governing by Design

A design system that didn't just ship — it became policy.

Design Systems Service Design Policy Government

Ministry of Finance, Republic of Indonesia. Led end-to-end design and regulatory codification of a ministry-wide design system governing digital communications across 13 Echelon I units serving 270M+ citizens.

Gold — IDMA 2024 Gold — PR Indonesia 2023 1st Social Media — AMH 2022

One of the very few design systems in Indonesia — government or private sector — to achieve binding policy status. Adoption did not depend on organizational culture or individual champions. It became enforceable.

4Policy Instruments
13Echelon I Units
25+Official Accounts
01Context & Problem

Trust through consistency — or the lack of it

Indonesia's Ministry of Finance oversees 13 Echelon I units — from Direktorat Jenderal Pajak to DJBC to DJPb — each managing independent digital platforms and public communications serving over 270 million citizens. By 2018, the visual output across these units had no shared standard.

  • Limited design-literate manpower. Most communications teams were not staffed by designers, producing inconsistent output based on individual judgment.
  • High rotation of personnel (mutasi). The civil service rotation system meant teams changed regularly. Each new contributor framed their own interpretations as innovation, compounding inconsistency.
  • Erosion of institutional credibility. Visually inconsistent communications gave the public genuine reason to question legitimacy. In an era of rampant misinformation, this had direct policy implications.
02Discovery & Insights
"We each have our own interpretation of what Kemenkeu looks like. There's no single source of truth."
"New staff who join after a rotation just base their work on whatever files they find in the shared folder — sometimes from years ago."

Key insight: design systems fail when adoption is voluntary. The only enforcement mechanism with real staying power in a large government institution is formal policy.

03Key Decisions
Decision 01

Two parallel tracks — social media and website — built on shared foundations. Unit-specific implementation within a unified visual language.

Decision 02

Build for the non-designer contributor first. Every specification written to be actionable by a communications officer on rotation.

Decision 03

Pursue policy codification, not just documentation. Translated design principles into regulatory language requiring legal review and ministerial endorsement.

Decision 04

Progressive specificity: web UI (2018) → brand identity (2020) → unit logos (2020) → physical identity (2021).

04Outcome & Impact
4Binding policy instruments
13Echelon I units governed
25+Official digital accounts
1300+Content/year under standard

The #UangKita campaign — built on this design system — became a sustained hashtag ecosystem with millions of engagements, demonstrating that policy-enforced consistency can generate organic brand recognition at scale.

05Reflection

Policy codification solved the adoption problem but introduced a structural challenge: design systems need to evolve, but policy moves slowly. The ministry's structure has since expanded (Perpres 158/2024), platform landscapes have shifted, and design trends have moved — none of which were anticipated in the original regulatory framework.

The deeper gap is enforcement architecture. The policy creates obligation but no reward or consequence mechanism. Building a monitoring system and defined review cycle into the regulatory framework from the start would have made the system more self-correcting over time.

Case Study / 02

Simulasi APBN

Redesigning how Indonesia's youth understand where their money goes.

UX Design Civic Tech Game Design Public Education
simulasiapbn.kemenkeu.go.id
11M+Impressions across platforms
3–5 minAverage session duration
11M+Impressions
3–5minAvg session
18–32Primary age
As featured in
KompasIndonesia's largest national newspaper
tvOneNational broadcast network
01Context & Problem

A literacy gap that infographics couldn't close

According to OJK-BPS SNLIK 2024, Indonesia's national financial literacy index stands at 65.43% — more than 1 in 3 citizens lacks sufficient financial knowledge. Among youth aged 18–25, the rate reaches only 70%, and understanding of how state budget allocation decisions are made is significantly lower still.

The Ministry of Finance communicates APBN data extensively. Yet the information remained inaccessible to people without an economics background. The challenge was not a lack of information — it was a fundamental mismatch between how data was presented and how the target audience learns. The Minister of Finance identified this gap directly and requested a solution that would meet Indonesia's youth on their own terms.

02Discovery & Insights
"There are rules when you organize the state budget — you can't freely adjust."
— State budget organizer interview
  • Budget items have constitutional floors (education 20%, health 5%, regional transfers) — these had to be modeled accurately, not simplified away
  • Most youth understood APBN exists but had no mental model of how allocation trade-offs are made
  • "Being the Minister of Finance" was immediately engaging — converting passive reception into active agency
  • Mobile-first was non-negotiable: most users would arrive via social media links
03Key Decisions
Decision 01 — Sliders

Sliders over input fields. Makes constraints visible — the floor, ceiling, range — translating legal budget structure into tactile, immediately understandable interaction.

Decision 02 — No Login

Reducing friction over data collection. 11M+ impressions validates this — a login wall would have significantly reduced reach.

Decision 03 — Full Rebuild

Phase 2 was a fundamental rebuild, not a patch. Landscape-only locked out mobile users. The system needed complete responsive redesign.

Decision 04 — Feedback Loop

Explicit feedback mechanism built into Phase 1 launch strategy, creating a cycle from public use → insight → iteration — rare for government digital products.

04Outcome & Impact
11M+Impressions
3–5minAvg session
18–32Primary age range
7yr+Sustained active use

Actively used in Kemenkeu Goes to Campus events and university collaborations through 2025. Featured in Kompas (2018, 2019) and tvOne (2024) as a civic innovation example.

05Reflection

The biggest design constraint — legal accuracy of the algorithm — turned out to be the biggest educational asset. When users hit a budget floor they couldn't override, the game taught something no infographic could: being "Minister of Finance" is far more constrained than it sounds.

A hypothetical Phase 3 would add a comparison layer showing the user's version alongside the actual enacted APBN, and a social mechanic that prompts deliberation rather than just result sharing.

Case Study / 03

Dana IndonesiaRaya

Mapping what a cultural endowment program actually needs to work.

Service Design Service Blueprint Gov Tech LPDP

Redesign of the digital reporting infrastructure for Indonesia's national cultural endowment grant program — from fragmented open-text submissions to a structured, evaluable, aggregatable service.

1,601,069Cultural appreciators reached
36,201Cultural practitioners supported
DAKB / LPDP Kemenkeu 2025

Redesign of the digital reporting infrastructure for Indonesia's national cultural endowment grant program — from fragmented open-text submissions to a structured, evaluable, aggregatable service.

1,144Funded Programs
1.6M+Cultural Audiences
36K+Practitioners
Artifacts Delivered
→ End-to-end service blueprint
→ 20-section structured input form
→ Verifier scoring framework
→ Dashboard architecture spec
→ Developer handoff documentation
01Context & Problem

A program producing impact — but not producing usable data

Dana IndonesiaRaya is Indonesia's cultural endowment grant program, managed by LPDP under the Ministry of Finance's Dana Abadi Kebudayaan — one of the few government-funded cultural preservation mechanisms in Southeast Asia backed by a permanent capital endowment of Rp5 trillion.

  • Grantees reported through unstructured formats — producing incomparable reports across grantees in the same category
  • Verifiers assessed without standardized scoring criteria, carrying evaluation knowledge in their heads
  • Administrators had no aggregated view of completion rates or cross-program patterns
  • Data could not be rolled up for institutional reporting or policy evidence
02Discovery & Insights
Verifiers spent disproportionate time on administrative disambiguation — trying to understand what the grantee meant — rather than on substantive evaluation. The input form was producing ambiguity as its primary output.

Service mapping revealed four critical failure points: unstructured grantee input, implicit verifier criteria, opaque handoffs (coordination through WhatsApp and email), and no aggregation layer. The grant reporting process existed as implicit knowledge distributed across email threads and individual memory.

03Key Decisions
Decision 01 — Structured Input

Every section redesigned with guided input — dropdowns, conditionals, numeric fields with unit labels. Open text preserved only where narrative was genuinely required.

Decision 02 — Scoring Rubric

Explicit scoring framework with defined criteria, dimensional weights, and anchors — making evaluation consistent and auditable across verifiers.

Decision 03 — Auto-calculation

Where scores derive from inputs (budget realization, output completion), the system calculates automatically — reducing error and increasing verifier confidence.

Decision 04 — Stage Separation

Grantee submission and verifier evaluation designed as distinct service stages with explicit handoff triggers — replacing informal coordination with a visible workflow.

04Outcome & Impact

Implementation handoff completed July 2025. Service blueprint and input form adopted as the basis for system development. Verifier scoring framework approved by program leadership. Before blueprinting, the grant reporting process existed as implicit knowledge — the blueprint made it auditable, improvable, and transferable regardless of personnel rotation.

05Reflection

This project clarified something fundamental about service design in government: the most significant design problems are rarely about the interface. They are about the handoffs — the implicit knowledge in people's heads, the gap between how a process is documented and how it actually operates.

Making that gap visible through service blueprinting created more value, before a single screen was designed, than any UI decision made afterward. For public sector service design, this is where the leverage lives.

Case Study / 04

StoryPost

When every office is a newsroom, the infrastructure has to work.

Product Design UX Design Internal Tool Gov Tech
storypost · internal · kemenkeu.go.id
2022Live since, actively maintained
SSOAll ministry employees, zero registration
Ministry of Finance Internal Stock Images Web App

An internal stock image web-based platform purpose-built for the Ministry of Finance — accessible to all employees through SSO, designed to make every regional office a contributor to a shared visual intelligence layer.

SSOEmployee identity integration
MonthlyChallenge cycles
2 ModeContribute & consume
Admin CMS Layer
storypost · admin panel
01Context & Problem

Every office generates content. Almost none of it was reachable.

Indonesia's Ministry of Finance operates through a nationwide network of vertical offices stretching from Sabang to Merauke. Each unit generates visual content daily: ministerial visits, field inspections, public events, budget socialization activities. But the infrastructure for capturing, organizing, and distributing this imagery was fragmented and slow.

When a unit needed quality photography for a publication or campaign, a request went to the central communications team in Jakarta, who would dispatch a photographer. This created a bottleneck that was expensive, slow, and unscalable — particularly for simultaneous events across multiple regions or time-sensitive publications that could not wait for central deployment.

The response was StoryPost, an internal stock image web-based platform purpose-built for the Ministry of Finance — accessible to all employees through SSO, designed to make every regional office a contributor to a shared visual intelligence layer.

02Discovery & Insights

The core user tension sat between two very different behaviors. Contributors — field staff uploading photos — needed a low-friction, mobile-friendly submission flow. Most would be uploading from their phones immediately after an event. Consumers — communications teams and publication editors — needed powerful search and filtering to quickly find the right image under deadline pressure.

Without a structured incentive or prompt, contribution would be sporadic and the database would stagnate. The challenge system was the design response to the cold-start problem.

A secondary insight: government photography routinely lacks metadata. Without systematic tagging at the point of upload, images become unsearchable within months. The upload flow was designed to make metadata entry feel like a natural part of the contribution process, not a separate obligation.

03Key Decisions
SSO as Trust Foundation

Integration with the ministry's employee SSO meant every contributor is verified, every image is attributable, and onboarding friction is eliminated entirely.

Dual-mode Architecture

Browsing and download experience kept visually and navigationally distinct from the contribution experience to prevent confusion between modes.

Challenge System as Engine

Monthly topics set by admin prompt contribution, create thematic coherence, and give communications teams predictable content to plan around.

CMS as Backbone

Admin layer designed for communications team to manage without technical support: topic creation, moderation, user management, contribution analytics by unit and region.

04Outcome & Impact

StoryPost has been live since 2022 and is actively maintained with monthly topic cycles. SSO integration is fully implemented across the ministry. Communications teams across Echelon I units can now access verified, attributable photography without deploying central photographers for routine regional events. The image database grows continuously through the challenge-driven contribution cycle.

05Reflection

The challenge system solved the cold-start problem but surfaced a new one: contribution rates are uneven across units. Region-specific challenges calibrated to local event calendars, or a nudge system tied to unit analytics, would be the natural next iteration.

Designing for contribution at institutional scale is fundamentally different from designing for consumption. The contribution flow has to compete with every other priority a busy civil servant has on any given day. It has to earn that place, not assume it.

Case Study / 05

G20 & ASEAN Chairmanship

Two presidencies, two years, one chance to get it right.

Service Design Digital Communications Multilateral High-Stakes Delivery

Indonesia held both the G20 Presidency (2022) and ASEAN Chairmanship (2023) in consecutive years. As part of the Ministry of Finance digital communications team, responsible for content strategy, web operations, and UX governance across both finance track digital presences.

1.5M+International users, G20 finance track
40%Web traffic increase, ASEAN peak events
g20.org Finance Track ASEAN 2023 Channels Tourism Integration
01Context & Problem

Service design operating at diplomatic scale

The G20 and ASEAN are annual forums that rotate chairmanship among member countries. In 2022 and 2023, Indonesia held the host role for both: the G20 Presidency under "Recover Together, Recover Stronger," and the ASEAN Chairmanship under "ASEAN Matters: Epicentrum of Growth." Hosting both in consecutive years made Indonesia the focal point of two of the world's most significant multilateral economic forums within a twelve-month window.

For the Ministry of Finance's digital communications team, this translated into a compounding operational challenge: how do you design, manage, and sustain digital content services for a global audience, coordinating across delegations from more than ten countries, under live-event conditions where errors are visible to international press and policymakers in real time?

What appeared to be a content management challenge was, at its core, a service design problem operating at diplomatic scale — where every handoff, every publication workflow, and every dependency between teams had to be explicitly mapped and managed. Both presidencies also carried a deliberate tourism dimension: meeting venues were chosen to showcase Indonesia's regions, and the digital presence supported this dual mandate.

02Discovery & Insights
What looked like a content problem was actually a service architecture problem. The core failure mode in high-stakes event communications is not poor writing or weak design. It is unclear ownership and unmanaged dependencies.

When a communiqué is finalized late during a finance ministers' meeting, the chain from document to published web content involves translation teams, legal review, delegation approval, web editors, and platform administrators across multiple organizations. Without explicit handoff protocols, this chain breaks under the exact conditions when it cannot afford to.

The key insight: high-stakes digital service delivery is not primarily about the quality of individual outputs. It is about the reliability of the process that produces them. From ASEAN 2023, a secondary insight emerged: the 40% traffic increase during peak events was driven not just by content quality but by the predictability of publication. Consistency of service is itself a form of content strategy.

03Key Decisions
Explicit Handoff Mapping

Publication workflow mapped end to end with named owners at each stage and defined escalation paths — reducing the informal coordination that had previously characterized event-driven publishing.

Tiered Content by Urgency

Outcomes documents and communiqués as Tier 1 (30 minutes). Background documents as Tier 2 (24 hours). This allowed the team to triage under pressure without debating priorities in real time.

Constraint over Prescription

Simplified constraint sets distributed as ready-to-use assets rather than exhaustive guidelines. Compliance came through removing the option to deviate, not through persuasion.

Tourism as Content Layer

Meeting venues, local cultural programs, and tour packages surfaced as editorial content alongside policy outputs — turning the event's physical footprint into place marketing for Indonesia.

04Outcome & Impact
1.5M+G20 international users
40%ASEAN traffic increase
10+Delegations coordinated
2yrConsecutive presidencies

Both presidencies positioned Indonesia's meeting venues as destinations in their own right. Labuan Bajo, Yogyakarta, Manado, and Bali were showcased through the digital presence as part of a broader narrative about Indonesia's geographic and cultural richness — turning diplomatic visibility into soft economic promotion that extended impact beyond formal policy outputs.

05Reflection

The hardest design problem in high-stakes event communications is not the visible one — the platform, the content, the brand. It is the invisible one: the workflow, the dependencies, the human handoffs that happen late at night when a minister has just signed a communiqué and the world is waiting.

Two presidencies, twelve months apart, taught one consistent lesson: explicit process is the most underrated design asset in high-complexity environments. The teams that performed best under pressure were not the ones with the best individual designers. They were the ones with the clearest handoffs.

02
Background

About

UX and service designer with 9+ years embedded in Indonesia's public sector, designing digital services from inside government, not as an external consultant. Works at the intersection of practice and policy: mapping complex services, aligning cross-functional stakeholders, and embedding design thinking into the regulatory and institutional structures that make change durable.

Holds a Master of Design from UNSW Sydney (2025, Australia Awards Scholar), with research on how governments in the UK, Australia, and the Netherlands institutionalise design capacity through national design systems — moving design from a project-level activity to a governance capability. Practitioner and researcher by orientation, with a broader conviction that governments must develop the internal expertise, methods, and authority to lead their own digital transformation: building design sovereignty rather than perpetually depending on external actors.

Currently serves as Director of Institutional Cooperation at AIDIA (Indonesian DKV Professional Association), leading professional standards development and design industry advocacy for government designers across Indonesia. Additional training in Growth-Driven Design (HubSpot), Creative Economy Policy (British Council), and Leadership (UNSW).
